invariants.rule: Use sentence case for headings and titles. First letter capitalized, rest lowercase except proper nouns.
invariants.context: Title guidelines: Use sentence case for both text and headings.

invariants.rule: Use imperative mood for scenario-based titles and verb-phrase headings (e.g., Create a new project, Deploy your application).
Do not prefix titles with How to.
Titles must be unique and unambiguous across the documentation. Do not rely on parent page names.
invariants.context: Title guidelines: Must be unique and unambiguous.

invariants.rule: Optimize titles for search. Use words a user would search for.
Use existing capitalized noun phrases for feature names: Page Builder, Email Builder, Form Builder, Continuous Integration, etc.
invariants.context: Title guidelines: Use existing noun phrases.

invariants.rule: Prefer active voice. Use passive only when: (a) active would blame the user, (b) emphasizing the receiver of an action, (c) the actor is not important.
Use present simple tense.
Use American English spelling (e.g., color not colour).
Use consistent terminology. Avoid unnecessary synonyms. The same term every time for UI elements, code, and specific terminology.
Avoid the pronoun it. Restate the actual subject for clarity. Pronouns must have clear antecedents and should never leave the paragraph they were introduced in.
Use indicative and imperative mood. Avoid subjunctive mood of verbs.
Use gender-neutral they even for singular usage.
Be inclusive. Do not use culturally specific idioms, slang, holiday references, or sports metaphors.
Eliminate redundancy. Avoid repetitive ideas and information already covered. Refer to earlier sections when necessary.
Replace long phrases with single words (because not due to the fact that, if not in the event that, use not utilize).
Avoid filler words: actually, basically, really, just, in fact. Remove unless they add necessary emphasis.
Write short sentences. Keep sentences under 20 words when possible.
Be specific. Replace vague terms with specific ones.
Be direct rather than polite. Do not use the word please.
invariants.context: Language: Be direct rather than polite. This includes not using the word please.

invariants.rule: Keep it simple and concise. Focus on what is necessary. Get to the point fast. Lead with what is important.
Use simple English. Readers may not be native speakers.
Do not add (s) to nouns for optional plurals. Plural contains singular. Write Wait 5 minutes not Wait 5 minute(s).
Lead with conditions/circumstances, then instructions. Example: To delete the document, select Delete (not Select Delete if you want to delete).
Never assume users have read a specific page. Any page could be the user first page and needs to work on its own. Link richly to other pages.
invariants.context: Language: Never assume users have read X.

invariants.rule: Title must not end with -ing form.
Use Select for buttons, check boxes, drop-down lists, radio buttons, items. Do not use Click.
Use Clear for unchecking check boxes. Do not use Uncheck.
Use Enter for typing/inserting values. Do not use Type.
Use Open/Close for applications and UI elements.
Use Go to for tabs, applications, URLs, UI locations.
Use Press only for keyboard interactions.
Do not use Click (input-specific). Use Select instead.
Do not use Enable/Disable for specific UI elements. Use Select/Clear for check boxes. Enable/disable is allowed for general functionality.
Do not use Check/Uncheck. Use Select/Clear.
Do not use Choose. Use Select instead.
Prefer action-focused instructions over UI-focused ones: Save the settings instead of Select the Save button.
Use You need to or The conditions must be fulfilled. Avoid You must/should/can/could (implies uncertainty). Exception: You can is OK for optional steps.
Avoid must when addressing users directly. Must is acceptable when not writing about people.
Avoid have to because have may mean something different. Not suitable for non-native speakers.
Use common contractions (it is, you are, that is, do not) for friendly, informal tone.
Avoid awkward contractions: there would, it will, they would.
Never use double contractions.
Never form a contraction from a noun and a verb (e.g., Microsoft is developing).
Do not mix contractions and spelled-out equivalents in the same UI text.
Use arrow characters (->) for navigation paths: Settings -> Security -> Users.
Do not mix different UI element types in arrow notation.
Use only well-known, existing abbreviations. Do not invent abbreviations.
Use etc. instead of three dots (...) except for expand box labels.
Prefer X of Y over Y apostrophe-s X for readability, especially with inanimate objects.
In guides, avoid referring to the material as this guide or In this guide. Focus on actions/results. Use this material if absolutely necessary.
Avoid jargon and complex expressions in guides. Replace metaphors (spin up with create, leverage with use).
Every documentation article must contain: Title, Introduction, Body, Result, and Next Steps.
Write an introduction at the beginning of each article providing context, real-world scenarios, and explaining value to the user.
Introduction must answer: What is the primary use case? Why does it benefit me? When should I use it?
For longer articles, add a Key points tip box with 3-5 bullet points summarizing the article. Do not make it a paragraph.
Key points callout: avoid excessive linking. Use sparingly and only when absolutely necessary.
Use ordered lists for step-by-step instructions. Use bullet points when order does not matter.
Write one action per step for business users. For developer audience, you may merge two related easy steps.
Aim for around 8 steps per section. If instructions exceed 8 steps, split into subsections with distinct headings.
Describe the end result at the end of each section so readers can confirm they followed instructions correctly.
Provide next steps at the end of articles, linking to related articles or additional resources.
Articles should be independent of other pages. Exceptions must be properly linked and explained.
invariants.context: Documentation page: should be independent of other pages.

invariants.rule: Assume a happy path. User has proper permissions and correct project setup. Mention prerequisites when necessary.
Avoid redundant introduction sentences after headings when the heading already specifies the scenario.
Use headings with general terms rather than Kentico-specific terminology.
Use sentence case for all list items (first letter capitalized).
invariants.context: Body: Use sentence case for lists.

invariants.rule: Use lists and tables to break up long paragraphs and improve readability.
Use the task-oriented approach (document what scenarios users can accomplish), not feature-oriented.
Avoid over-explaining common concepts. Assume basic familiarity unless audience requires beginner-level guidance.
Reference and example pages: title with Reference - XYZ or Example - XYZ. Prefer embedding on main page; separate pages only when original is too long or relates to multiple pages.
Lists must not exceed 3 levels deep.
Each guide must have a clear type label: Concept, Tutorial, or How-to (following Diataxis framework).
Guides introduction: brief 1-2 sentence summary, define learning outcome in real-world business context, show screenshot/GIF of expected output, list prerequisites.
Keep guides short and focused (A to Z completion). Break long content into multi-step scenarios.
Always include a table of contents (TOC) markup in guides.
invariants.context: Use a table of contents markup.

invariants.rule: Paragraphs in guides: 3-5 sentences maximum. Sections should be less than 3 paragraphs for longer materials.
Do not rely on page continuity. Use self-contained phrasing. Each section must work if reader jumps in mid-guide.
Each guide in a series must include a Next step section with a clear heading. Use a dedicated heading so it can be removed for module reuse.
invariants.context: Lead users to the next step.

invariants.rule: Wrap non-module content (prerequisites, next steps, recaps) in dedicated sections for easy removal during module creation.
Each guide belongs to exactly one section in the Learn portal guides hierarchy.
Encourage learners to try examples on their own. Include a self-assessment element.
invariants.context: Help users review what they have learned.

invariants.rule: Use bold for UI element names: application names, tabs, buttons, fields, dialog names.
For repeated UI elements in the same paragraph, use italics for second and subsequent mentions.
Use italics for field values, object names being edited, and site selector values.
Use backticks for inline code: API members, configuration keys, methods, namespaces, classes, command line tools and arguments.
Do not use backticks for database table/column names.
Never pluralize code elements directly. Use noun phrases: IUserInfoProvider classes.
Do not add bold or italics to inline code-highlighted text.
Generally avoid underlining. Use only when describing underlined UI elements.
Use ALL CAPS only for emphasis (NOT, AND, OR) and standard abbreviations (ID, GUID).
Punctuation after formatted text must not be bold or italic.
Use one space between sentences and words. Use 4 spaces for indentation, no tabs or 2-space indents.
Use the serial (Oxford) comma for clarity.
Always use a comma after for example, e.g., i.e.
Avoid semicolons when possible. Split sentences instead.
Use hyphens (-) without spaces for compound modifiers (out-of-the-box feature).
Use en dash for ranges (1975-1980), field descriptions, and emphasis. Do not use em dash at all.
Use straight quotes. Punctuation goes inside quotation marks (American English style).
URLs use forward slashes. File system paths use backslashes. Relative paths use forward slashes with tilde.
Use a.m./p.m. (lowercase with periods) for time. Close format for temperature: 25C, 175F. Ordinals without spaces or hyphens: 1st, 2nd, 3rd.
In release notes files: use italics for text that would normally be bold. Use bold only for important emphasis.
Combine action and result in one sentence when possible for steps: Select Edit to open the dialog.
Filenames must be lowercase, hyphenated (kebab-case), reflecting page titles. Filenames determine URL slugs.
Screenshot filenames use snake_case and PNG format only.
Use callout types in order of severity: tip (bonus info), info (additional context), note (important points), warning (destructive actions/severe consequences).
Always include a bold title on a separate line in callouts, unless the content is a single sentence where the title would be redundant.
Do not write the callout type name inside the callout (e.g., do not write Note inside a note box).
Do not misuse callout types. Match severity to the content.
Every page must have YAML front matter between --- lines with required fields: title, identifier, order, redirect_from, persona, license (for _documentation and _guides collections).
Identifier must be snake_case base + collection suffix (_xp for _documentation, _guides for _guides). Must be unique across the whole repo.
Order field: start at 100 for new sections; insert by midpoints (550 between 500 and 600).
redirect_from must always include x/<identifier> plus any additional legacy paths.
License tiers: 1 (standard), 2 (advanced). Mandatory for _documentation and _guides collections.
Valid persona values: developer, admin, business, architect, all.
Identifier structure: unique code name + collection suffix (_guides, _xp, or _collection). Must be unique across the help service.
For section-specific licensing, use license_info Liquid tag instead of front matter. Content inside license tags is excluded from search indexing.
When inserting page_link, never guess the identifier. Always get it from the target file front matter. If the target file cannot be found, insert a TODO placeholder.
invariants.context: Create page links without hallucinating identifiers.

invariants.rule: Set the collection parameter in page_link based on source folder: _documentation -> documentation, _guides -> guides, _api -> api. Only required when referencing from a different collection.
When adding an anchor to page_link, only use an anchor that actually exists on the target page. Do not invent anchors.
Anchor IDs must start with a letter, contain no whitespace, and use only ASCII letters, digits, underscore, or hyphen.
External links default target is _blank. Include the URL scheme; bare domains are treated as relative.
Use descriptive link text. Avoid vague phrases like click here or this page.
In guides, include relevant links naturally within the body text at the point of need. Avoid using the Related Pages component at the end.
invariants.context: Avoid using the related pages component.

invariants.rule: Use suppress_warnings=true on page_link when intentionally linking to older/disabled collections.
Business users: friendly, positive tone. One action per step. No technical jargon. More screenshots for clarity.
Business users: assume they work on a team that includes technical people who handle setup tasks.
Developers and admins: can combine 2 related easy steps. Use developer terminology appropriately. Explain business/marketing terms when needed.
Adjust vocabulary specifically to the target audience. Do not use business user terminology for developers and vice versa. Explain cross-domain terms.
Guides tone: conversational, friendly, encouraging. Use you for personal tone, we/lets for teamwork.
Business user guide materials: provide real-world marketing scenarios. Reference KBank/Dancing Goat demos when possible.
Xperience by Kentico: first mention full name, subsequent Xperience. Use Kentico alone only for the company. Never use Kentico alone for the product.
Always: Open the XXX application. Never: Open the XXX app from the application list.
Use sign in (verb), sign-in (noun/adjective). Use sign in to (never sign into). Never use log in, login, log on.
check box: two words.
drop-down list: hyphenated.
radio button: two words.
user name: two words.
curly brackets: never braces.
scroll bar: two words.
Use minimum for quantities, minimal for probability or quality.
allowlist/denylist: use instead of whitelist/blacklist.
email: no hyphen.
online: no hyphen.
website: one word.
web server: two words.
blog post: two words.
lifecycle: one word.
startup: one word (noun).
timeout: one word (noun).
plugin: one word, no hyphen.
client-side and server-side: always hyphenated as qualifier.
front end / back end: two words; hyphenated when qualifying nouns (back-end development).
file system: two words (not filesystem).
microsite, multichannel, multilingual: one word, no hyphens.
Do not use .NET Core. It has been replaced by .NET.
Internet capitalized as a noun; lowercase internet as an adjective.
Treat data as singular (data is, not data are).
third party: two words (noun); third-party: hyphenated (adjective).
e-commerce is deprecated: use commerce. online marketing is deprecated: use Digital marketing.

User types: user = admin account holder; member = registered visitor without admin access; visitor = anonymous website user; contact = Xperience entity for visitors. Do not confuse contact with visitor or customer.
Use visitor over customer for website interaction. Use customer only in commerce contexts.
Avoid end user when possible; use specific terms (developers, business users).
SaaS is not a proper noun. Use with the definite article the (the SaaS environment). When qualifying: SaaS project, SaaS configuration.
private cloud: common noun, no capitalization.
Xperience Portal: proper noun, no article.
live site: two words; hyphenated when qualifying (live-site development).
step-by-step: always hyphenated.
Feature names: capitalize Form Builder, Page Builder, Email Builder, Continuous Integration, Continuous Deployment. Lowercase marketing in Digital marketing.
Admin terminology: administration (full: Xperience administration), admin UI (developer docs), administration interface (non-technical users), application (unit in admin UI).
Clone vs Copy: clone = create duplicate in admin UI listings; copy = add to clipboard for pasting (Page Builder widgets).
GIFs must be under 5 seconds. Longer content should be saved as video format.
Add alt text and captions for all visuals to make them accessible.
Do not list generic prerequisites (MVC, .NET Core, constructor injection). Focus on XbyK-specific knowledge.
Address common customer pitfalls with WARN callouts in guides.
Include realistic business examples even in POC-level guides. Avoid generic or artificial examples.
Avoid using in Xperience by Kentico in module/path names. Use verb-title in base infinitive form (not -ing).
Module intro text must not exceed 152 characters.
Remove all unnecessary comments before merging modules/paths.
on-site: hyphenated when qualifying nouns.
register is preferred over sign up for account creation.
Screenshots: hide AIRA panel, exclude left sidebar via clip offset, crop dead space at bottom.
Screenshot highlights use red outline (3px solid red) with 3px offset.
Screenshots must be saved to src/_docsassets/<collection>/<target-folder>/.
Visuals must be self-explanatory and standalone. Use notes, comments, legend, and proper sizing.
Use Liquid admonition tags: tip, info, note, warning, key.

Code blocks use the code Liquid tag with supported lang values: bash, bicep, c, css, csharp, cshtml, git, graphql, js, java, json, jsx, html, markdown, nginx, powershell, tsx, regex, sql, typescript, yaml. Use lang=text for no highlighting.
Images use the image Liquid tag with path src/_docsassets/<collection>/<pagefilename>/. Use border=true for UI/screenshot images.
Files use the file Liquid tag with path src/_docsassets/<collection>/<pagefilename>/. Disposition: download (default) or open.
Video: YouTube URLs must use /embed/ format.
Columns layout: up to 4 columns, responsive.
Card component: use either icon OR image, not both.
Banner modes: primary (default) or secondary.
Related pages in front matter must be from same collection.
Inline TOC: only minHeadingLevel and maxHeadingLevel params supported inline.
Table markup uses table/row/cell Liquid tags with header, colspan, rowspan options.
Use key macro for key takeaways in guides.
When transforming guides into modules, create an admonition using module_link macro.
code_link tag requires source and lang attributes. Lang values must be from supported list.
Section markers in code_link use language-specific comment syntax: // for C#/TS/JS/CSS/JSON, HTML comments for HTML/XML, -- for SQL, # for YAML/Bash/PowerShell.
Use //Include: / //EndInclude: markers to demarcate extractable code regions in the codesamples project.
After every update to codesamples project files, run npm run codesamples:build to verify the project compiles.
When adding a new feature area to codesamples, create a new top-level folder following the feature-folder organization pattern.
Code comments: use third person singular. Omit subject when clear. Use punctuation in multi-line comments. No final dot for single-line comments. Whitespace after comment symbol.
If a code example has 4 or more parameters in a constructor/attribute, use named parameter syntax for readability.
For incomplete code examples, use ellipsis (three manually written dots). Do not write ellipsis inside comments.
Write ID and GUID as Id and Guid in source code (not comments), treating them as words.
Comment only what is important. Do not comment code that is easy to understand.
Developer guide code: focus comments on Kentico-specific methods, not generic .NET. Link to MS documentation for generic .NET concepts.
Developer guides: break code examples into logical steps/sections. Tutorials need more detailed code; How-tos need less.
Draw.io diagrams: use Bootstrap library for UI elements, Material Design for icons.
Draw.io color palette: #AF00FA (shapes), #BCA39A (connectors), #000000/#FFFFFF (text).
Draw.io font: GT Walsheim Pro only. Set fontFamily to GT Walsheim in generated XML.
